note: Captions are working placeholders written from the collection catalog and the holding institutions' records. Dates marked as catalog dates or unverified have not been checked against the sheet imprint.
wanted: Narvaez, Plano del Territorio de la Alta California 1830 (digitized on Calisphere, blocked to scripts, needs a manual browser save)
1839 Carta esferica de la costa de la Alta California (CSUMB Hornbeck, gated, manual save)
Higher resolution Costanso 1770 (1331 px is the John Carter Brown Library's full size; a larger scan would need another holder)
Kotzebue and Choris, chart of San Francisco Bay from the Rurik voyage, 1816 (not on Rumsey; check LOC and Commons)
Tanner, Map of the United States of Mexico, earlier edition (copy in collection has an unverified edition and is held back)
A Russian-made chart of Ross or Bodega (RAC/Kuskov era) — none found openly digitized; check RGAVMF/Gibson plates

groups.label: The 1775 Expedition Charts
groups.intro: Manuscript charts from the 1775 Hezeta and Bodega y Quadra expedition, the voyage that discovered Bodega Bay, plus related Spanish manuscript charts. The Library of Congress acquired this collection in 2024, so several of these charts may be unrecorded in Wagner's standard cartobibliography.

groups.label: The Spanish Hydrographic Atlas (1799)
groups.intro: A 25 sheet manuscript atlas, Cartas esfericas y planos de los puertos, the Spanish Navy's synthesis of its Pacific coast port surveys from 1775 to 1796. Sheet identifications follow the Library of Congress contents list and sheet order. Where a sheet's own title has not been read yet, the caption says so.

groups.label: Spanish and Mexican California
groups.intro: Spanish colonial cartography of Alta California and the maps the independent Mexican republic made of it.

groups.label: Russian California and the Duflot de Mofras Atlas
groups.intro: The cartography of the Russian presence at Fort Ross and Bodega, mostly through Duflot de Mofras, the French attache whose 1844 atlas recorded the coast in the last years of Mexican rule. The group also holds the surviving eyewitness views of Ross and Bodega, Russian and French, since no Russian-made map of the colony is openly digitized yet.

groups.label: Foreign Surveys
groups.intro: British, French, and American naval surveys of the California coast. For most of its existence Spanish and Mexican California was better charted by foreign navies than by its own government.

groups.label: Claims and Boundary Maps
groups.intro: The maps empires argued with. American, British, and Mexican maps drawing competing claims across the continent, from Drake's New Albion to the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo.

groups.label: Conquest and Gold Rush
groups.intro: The American arrival on paper. Military reconnaissance, the first US government maps of the ceded territory, and the first American surveys of California's towns and harbors.

items.title: Plano del Puerto del Capitan Bodega
items.maker: Bodega y Quadra and Francisco Mourelle
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Manuscript chart of Bodega Bay, drawn by Bodega y Quadra and his pilot Francisco Mourelle on the voyage that discovered it. Keyed points mark Punta del Cordon, Punta de Arenas, and Punta de Murguia, with soundings at the entrance. This is the founding map of the Spanish claim to the port.

items.title: Carta reducida de las costas y mares septentrionales de la California
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: The large general chart of the 1775 voyage, 130 by 81 cm in the original. It carries the whole track of the expedition up the coast.

items.title: Carta reducida (small version)
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: The small version of the same general chart, 40 by 24 cm in the original.

items.title: Carta reducida (Hezeta)
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Hezeta's own general chart of the 1775 expedition, 68 by 45 cm in the original. Hezeta commanded the voyage; Bodega y Quadra commanded the schooner Sonora.

items.title: Carta reducida (Hezeta, small version)
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: The small version of Hezeta's chart, 40 by 28 cm in the original.

items.title: Plano del Puerto de la Trinidad
items.maker: Hezeta, Bodega y Quadra, and Mourelle
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Chart of Trinidad harbor at 41 degrees 7 minutes, where the expedition raised a cross and took possession in June 1775.

items.title: Plano de la Bahia de la Asumpcion o entrada de Ezeta
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: The Spanish discovery chart of the Columbia River mouth, drawn seventeen years before Robert Gray crossed the bar and named the river. Hezeta sighted the entrance in August 1775 but could not enter it.

items.title: Plano de la Rada de Bucareli
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Hezeta's chart of an anchorage at 47 degrees 24 minutes on the Washington coast.

items.title: Plano de la entrada o Puerto de Bucareli
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: The port at 55 degrees 17 minutes in southeast Alaska, the farthest major survey of the Sonora's push north.

items.title: Plano del Puerto de los Remedios
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Chart of the port at 57 degrees 18 minutes, near the northern limit of the 1775 voyage.

items.title: Carta reducida entre el grado 36 y el 61
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Bodega y Quadra's summary chart of both his northern voyages, 1775 and 1779. The cartouche names the ships Hezeta, Sonora, Princesa, and Favorita.

items.title: Plano del Puerto Sidman
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Spanish manuscript chart of a port reconnoitered by Captain Baker of Vancouver's expedition, evidence of how quickly Spanish cartographers absorbed British survey work.

items.title: Plano del puerto de San Diego
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Pantoja's chart of San Diego harbor, the base survey behind most later Spanish charts of the port.

items.title: Carta de los descubrimientos de la costa N.O. (19th century copy)
items.maker: unknown copyist, after an original of the early 1790s
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: A manuscript copy, dated by the Library of Congress to about 1850, of a chart of the northwest coast discoveries dedicated to Viceroy Revilla Gigedo. Useful for content, but it is a copy, not a period sheet.

items.title: Cartas esfericas, sheet 1: Carta reducida, Acapulco to Unalaska
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: The atlas's general chart, running the whole coast from Acapulco to Unalaska. Drawn under Bodega y Quadra in 1792. This is the largest sheet in the set and its identity is confirmed from the sheet.

items.title: Cartas esfericas, sheet 2: Puerto de Acapulco
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 2 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows Puerto de Acapulco, surveyed by Montes, 1796. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.title: Cartas esfericas, sheet 3: Puerto de San Blas
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 3 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows Puerto de San Blas, surveyed by Camacho, 1779. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.title: Cartas esfericas, sheet 4: Puerto de San Diego
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 4 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows Puerto de San Diego, surveyed by after the Vizcaino era surveys. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.title: Cartas esfericas, sheet 5: Santa Barbara and Purisima ensenadas, with Todos Santos
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 5 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows Santa Barbara and Purisima ensenadas, with Todos Santos, surveyed by Pantoja and Tovar, 1782. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.title: Cartas esfericas, sheet 6: Puerto de Monterey
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 6 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows Puerto de Monterey. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.title: Cartas esfericas, sheet 7: Puerto de San Francisco
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 7 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows Puerto de San Francisco, surveyed by Camacho, 1779. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.title: Cartas esfericas, sheet 8: Plano del Puerto de la Bodega
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Bodega Bay as resurveyed in 1793, the year of the failed Spanish attempt to occupy it. The title states the port was discovered by Bodega y Quadra in 1775 and reconnoitered in 1793. Identity confirmed from the sheet. The left margin of this file is soft because the Library of Congress high resolution derivative is corrupt and the sheet was rebuilt from tiles.

items.title: Cartas esfericas, sheet 9: Puerto de la Trinidad
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 9 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows Puerto de la Trinidad, surveyed by Hezeta, 1775. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.title: Cartas esfericas, sheet 10: Entrada de Ezeta o Rio de la Columbia
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 10 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows Entrada de Ezeta o Rio de la Columbia, surveyed by as reexamined 1793. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.title: Cartas esfericas, sheet 11: Puerto Grek
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 11 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows Puerto Grek, surveyed by Martinez y Zayas, 1793. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.title: Cartas esfericas, sheet 12: Interior channels, 48 to 50 degrees, with five port insets
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 12 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows Interior channels, 48 to 50 degrees, with five port insets, surveyed by 1791 surveys. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.title: Cartas esfericas, sheet 13: Strait of Juan de Fuca
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 13 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows Strait of Juan de Fuca, surveyed by 1793. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.title: Cartas esfericas, sheet 14: Nitinat
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 14 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows Nitinat, surveyed by Carrasco. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 15 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows Clayocuat. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.title: Cartas esfericas, sheet 16: Coast from Futusi to San Francisco
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 16 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows Coast from Futusi to San Francisco, surveyed by 1793. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 17 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows Nutka. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.title: Cartas esfericas, sheet 18: San Lorenzo de Nuca
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 18 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows San Lorenzo de Nuca, surveyed by Malaspina expedition, 1791. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 19 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows Puerto Gaston. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 20 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows Puerto Floridablanca. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.title: Cartas esfericas, sheet 21: Puerto Bazan
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 21 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows Puerto Bazan. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 22 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows Puerto de los Dolores. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 23 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows Puerto de Bucarely. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 24 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows Puerto de la Regla, surveyed by 1779. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.title: Cartas esfericas, sheet 25: Puerto de Santiago
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: Sheet 25 of the Spanish Navy's manuscript atlas of Pacific port surveys, 1775 to 1796. Per the contents list it shows Puerto de Santiago, surveyed by 1779. The identification follows the Library of Congress contents list and the sheet order, and has not been confirmed from the sheet itself.

items.title: Plano of the Pueblo San Jose suertes
items.credit: The Bancroft Library, University of California, Berkeley, via Internet Archive. BANC MSS C-A 52.
items.caption: Plan of the farm plots distributed to the settlers of the Pueblo San Jose, dated April 23, 1782, over Jose Moraga's signature. Each suerte in the grid carries a settler's name, with the Rio de Guadalupe and the acequia drawn alongside. The original burned in 1906; this is the plan as copied into the Savage transcripts, the only surviving version.

items.title: Plano of the Pueblo de la Reina de los Angeles
items.maker: Savage transcript copy of the 1786 original
items.credit: The Bancroft Library, University of California, Berkeley, via Internet Archive. BANC MSS C-A 52.
items.caption: The earliest town plan of Los Angeles, from the pueblo's 1786 possession papers. It shows the plaza with the church marked by a cross, the grid of house lots and farm plots, the Rio de Porciuncula, and the Acequia Madre. The original burned in 1906; this is the Savage transcript copy. It pairs with the Ord and Hutton survey of 1849 in the Conquest section as the beginning and end of the pueblo era.

items.title: Audience de Guadalajara, Nouveau Mexique, Californie
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: A classic map showing California as an island, the error that dominated European cartography for a century. Included here as the starting point the later surveys corrected. The date comes from the catalog record.

items.title: Carta reducida del Oceano Asiatico o Mar del Sur
items.credit: John Carter Brown Library, Brown University. Open access.
items.caption: Costanso's synthesis map from the Portola expedition, dated Mexico, October 1770. This is the cartographic base of the colonization of Alta California, drawn by the engineer who marched with the founding expedition.

items.title: Plano del Puerto de San Francisco
items.credit: Wikimedia Commons. Public domain.
items.caption: The first map of San Francisco Bay, from the surveys Canizares made in 1775 and 1776. This is the engraved state published in 1781. The manuscript original is at the Bancroft Library.

items.title: Carta esferica de los reconocimientos hechos en la costa N.O.
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: From the published atlas of the Sutil and Mexicana voyage, Madrid 1802. This was Spain's public answer to Vancouver's atlas, its case that Spanish surveys had covered the coast first. Edition details come from the catalog record.

items.title: Carta esferica de los reconocimientos, second sheet
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: The companion sheet of the same published survey chart.

items.title: Carta esferica de los territorios de la alta y baja Californias y estado de Sonora
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: The young Mexican republic's frame for the Californias and Sonora, made in the year after independence. Hornbeck reproduces this same sheet as the standard image of Mexican California's administrative geography.

items.title: Plano del Puerto de San Francisco
items.credit: Stanford Libraries, Barry Lawrence Ruderman Map Collection.
items.caption: The Mexican republic's own chart of San Francisco Bay, engraved in Mexico in 1825. The outline descends from Canizares. The key lists the mission, Isla de Alcatraces, and the Farallones. The imprint was read from the sheet.

items.title: Carte detaillee du mouillage du Fort Ross et du Port de la Bodega ou Romanzoff
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: The most detailed period map of the Russian anchorages. It shows Fort Ross and Bodega harbor under their Russian name Romanzoff, drawn in the last years of the colony. Scanned at full resolution, so the soundings and shore details are all legible.

items.title: Carte de la cote de l'Amerique sur l'Ocean Pacifique Septentrional
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: Duflot's general map of the Pacific coast, covering Oregon, the Californias, and the Gulf of California. Plate details come from the catalog record.

items.title: Port de San Francisco dans la Haute Californie
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: Duflot's plan of San Francisco Bay on the eve of the American conquest.

items.title: Plan du Port et de la Baie de Monte-Rey
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: Duflot's plan of Monterey, the capital's harbor, with the Bay of Trinidad as a companion.

items.title: Carte du Rio Colombia
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: The Columbia River up to Fort Vancouver, the Hudson's Bay Company anchor of the coast Duflot was sizing up for France.

items.title: Plan de l'embouchure du Rio Colorado, with the Port of San Diego
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: The mouth of the Colorado and the port of San Diego on one plate, the southern edges of the territory.

items.title: Port de Quadra ou de la Decouverte, with the Plan du Port de Nutka
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: Two harbor plans on one plate, Port Quadra or Discovery and Nootka with the Cala de los Amigos. These are the Vancouver Island ports where Spain and Britain fought out the Nootka crisis of 1789 to 1794, redrawn by Duflot fifty years later.

items.title: Mouillage de San Pedro, with the Mouillage de la Mission de Santa Barbara
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: Duflot's anchorage plans for San Pedro and the Santa Barbara roadstead, numbers 12 and 13 of the atlas. These open roadsteads were the working ports of the southern hide trade.

items.title: Plan geometrique de la Mission de St. Louis Roi de France
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: The only measured ground plan of a California mission complex in Duflot's atlas, drawn at San Luis Rey in the middle of secularization. A rare architectural record of what was being dismantled.

items.title: A Chart Shewing Part of the Coast of N.W. America (New Albion sheet)
items.maker: George Vancouver, prepared under Lt. Joseph Baker
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: Vancouver's California sheet, lettered Part of the Coast of New Albion, with an inset of Trinidad Bay. The British claim language is on the map itself. New Albion was Drake's name, and using it on a 1798 chart of Spanish California was a statement.

items.title: Chart of the Coast of N.W. America, 30 degrees to 38 degrees 30 minutes north
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: The southern companion sheet, running from San Diego past the Golden Gate to the latitude of Bodega. The best foreign survey of the Alta California coast at the century's end.

items.title: General chart of the coast, 29 degrees 54 minutes to 58 degrees 52 minutes north
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: The general chart of Vancouver's whole survey, Baja California to Alaska.

items.title: Cote N.O. de l'Amerique (general sheet)
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: The general northwest coast sheet from the atlas of the La Perouse expedition, the French scientific voyage that visited Monterey in 1786.

items.title: Baie de Monterey
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: The French plan of Monterey Bay from the 1786 visit, the first foreign scientific expedition received in Spanish California.

items.title: The Harbour of San Francisco, Nueva California
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: The Royal Navy survey of San Francisco Bay from Beechey's 1826 to 1828 voyage. This stayed the standard chart of the bay into the 1840s, which says something about who was actually mapping Mexican California.

items.title: Map of the Oregon Territory
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: The US Exploring Expedition's map of the Oregon country, which reaches down into the San Francisco Bay and Sacramento country the squadron surveyed. It fed American ambitions for both Oregon and California.

items.title: Entrance, San Francisco
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: The American naval survey of the Golden Gate from the Wilkes expedition.

items.title: Carquines Straits, Vallejo Bay, and other anchorages
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: Wilkes's chart of the Carquinez Strait and the northern arms of the bay, ground the interactive maps here cross constantly.

items.title: An Accurate Map of North America
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: The standard British mid eighteenth century map of the continent, carrying New Albion on the Pacific coast. The Drake claim, kept alive in print for two hundred years.

items.title: A Map Exhibiting All the New Discoveries in the Interior Parts of North America
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: London's running state of knowledge map of the west and the northwest coast, the Hudson's Bay Company world. The edition of this copy has not been checked against the imprint.

items.title: Louisiana
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: The purchase era map of the Louisiana territory, from the Arrowsmith and Lewis atlas. What the United States thought it had just bought.

items.title: Carte generale du Royaume de la Nouvelle Espagne
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: Humboldt's general map of New Spain, the most authoritative published picture of Mexico's geography before independence. Every claims map of the next generation argued with this one.

items.title: Map of the United States with the Contiguous British and Spanish Possessions
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: American map of the continent. It colors the United States claim west to the Pacific, north of Spanish California, with Spanish possessions south of about 42 degrees. The coloring was read from this copy. Melish reissued the map constantly between 1816 and 1823 and the edition of this copy is unverified. The 42 degree line here anticipates the Adams Onis treaty boundary of 1819.

items.title: A Map of Mexico, Louisiana, and the Missouri Territory
items.credit: Library of Congress, Geography and Map Division. Public domain.
items.caption: The maximal American cartographic claim of the treaty moment, published in Philadelphia in 1819. Robinson drew the American case at its most aggressive just as the boundary was being negotiated.

items.title: Map of the United States of Mexico
items.credit: Stanford Libraries, Barry Lawrence Ruderman Map Collection.
items.caption: The war year edition of Tanner's standard American map of the Mexican republic. The Tanner plate fed directly into the treaty cartography arguments of 1848.

items.title: Mapa de los Estados Unidos de Mejico
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: The Disturnell map, the family of maps referenced by the Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo. Disturnell issued at least seven 1847 editions with differing boundaries, and the edition of this copy is unverified, which matters before making any boundary claim from it.

items.title: A New Map of Texas, Oregon and California
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: The annexation year synthesis, sold to emigrants and expansionists alike. Texas, Oregon, and California as one connected American question.

items.title: Mexico, the British Possessions in North America and the United States
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: London's view of the continental partition in the Oregon question era, on two sheets joined.

items.title: Military Reconnaissance of the Arkansas, Rio del Norte and Rio Gila
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: Emory's route map of the Army of the West's march to California, the conquest's own cartographic record.

items.title: Map of Oregon and Upper California
items.maker: John C. Fremont and Charles Preuss
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: The first US government map of the newly conquered territory, drawn by Preuss from Fremont's surveys.

items.title: Oregon, Upper California and New Mexico
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: The ceded conquest as first mapped for the American public, published in Philadelphia in 1849. Upper or New California still fills the whole interior basin. This copy replaces an earlier low resolution scan; the sheet is now fully readable at zoom.

items.title: Plan de la Ciudad de Los Angeles
items.maker: E. O. C. Ord and William Hutton
items.credit: Los Angeles Public Library, Tessa digital collection.
items.caption: The first survey of Los Angeles, August 1849. This is the hinge document between rancho land tenure and American property law, the sheet on which the pueblo's lands became lots. This scan is of a period copy held by the Los Angeles Public Library.

items.title: City of San Francisco and its Vicinity
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: The federal survey of the instant city.

items.title: Bodega Bay, California
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: The American chart of Bodega Bay. The port the Spanish discovered in 1775 and never held, surveyed at last by the power that ended up with it.

items.title: New Map of California
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: Gibbes's Gold Rush synthesis of the new state, including the Southern Mines country.

items.title: View of Fort Ross
items.credit: Wikimedia Commons. Public domain.
items.caption: The classic eyewitness image of Ross: blockhouses, chapel dome, and stockade on the terrace above the cove, drawn by the French trader Duhaut-Cilly on his 1828 visit. Not a map, but the best surviving picture of what the Spanish reconnaissance reports describe.

items.title: View of Bodega (Port Rumiantsev)
items.credit: Wikimedia Commons. Public domain.
items.caption: Duhaut-Cilly's eyewitness view of the Russian anchorage at Bodega in 1828. The port Spain discovered in 1775 and never held, as it looked under Russian use.

items.title: Settlement Ross, 1841
items.credit: Wikimedia Commons. Public domain.
items.caption: Ross in its final year before the sale to Sutter: the windmill and the village grown outside the stockade. Voznesensky was the Russian-American Company naturalist at Ross in 1840 and 1841; the attribution is conventional, not documented on the file.

items.title: Settlement Ross, 1841 (second version)
items.credit: Wikimedia Commons. Public domain.
items.caption: A second version of the same 1841 view of Ross. Same attribution caveat as its companion.

items.title: Sketch of the Chernykh Ranch
items.credit: Wikimedia Commons. Public domain.
items.caption: The Russian farm inland from Ross, sketched in the colony's last year. The agricultural spread beyond the stockade is what Vallejo's 1833 reconnaissance was tracking.

items.title: General Chart, San Francisco Bay
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: From Ringgold's series of charts with sailing directions, the first thorough American survey of the bay after the conquest, made for the Gold Rush shipping that Beechey's old chart could no longer serve.

items.title: San Pablo Bay and Carquines Straits
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: The strait's first American navigation chart. The narrows the Karkin once controlled, sounded for the river traffic to the gold country.

items.title: Suisun and Vallejo Bays
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: Ringgold's chart of Suisun Bay and the Vallejo anchorage, the water road to Sacramento. Solano County's shore in its first American survey.

items.title: Plan of the Harbour of San Francisco and its Vicinity
items.credit: Courtesy David Rumsey Map Collection, David Rumsey Map Center, Stanford Libraries. CC BY-NC-SA.
items.caption: A second Beechey sheet of the bay, companion to the harbour chart already in this group. The Royal Navy's survey remained the standard picture of the bay for two decades.
